speaker: interviewer
text: Number 3. Today is May 31, 1995. Survivor, Rose Schachter, Schachter. Interviewer, Rhoda Daum-Kenner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's continue with the morning after the night when you heard the shooting and were all alone locked in that room.

speaker: interviewee
text: And crying all night through. In the morning, the same driver appeared. And he took me with also a small truck to Rovno, which is one the chains. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was introduced there to the manager there, which is also from Hamburg. He was there with his wife, Paula. Fritz-- I have the name--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not Jewish, German?

speaker: interviewee
text: German.

speaker: interviewer
text: German.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody German. He was the head of this particular chain in Rovno. I have his name someplace, because somebody wrote a book about Grabe. And there's-- I testified to this book a short episode. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] When I arrived there, the people which-- Germans which worked there, actually, they knew me all, because being at the company had-- was a chain, they were traveling from one place to another. They were in Rogalin. They were all over. So they knew me. They knew I'm Jewish. But I had this piece of paper, false piece of paper, which I neglected to mention before that I got from my friend Mark [NON-ENGLISH]. He's the one I gave the address, you know, from-- he lives now in Jersey City. He's a chiropractor. He and another friend-- I'm going back a little.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, that's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: He and another friend, David, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they got hold of some papers from the town from the people which were dead, and they-- distribute-- Polish people. And they distributed between the Jews. And I got one of those papers under the name of Janina Bukowska.

speaker: interviewer
text: Janina?

speaker: interviewee
text: Janina, J-A-N-I-N-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the second name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bukowska, B-U-K-O-W-S-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said that you had those papers with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this piece of paper I had with me. I didn't have nothing else but this piece of paper which is so big-- no picture of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, another luck-- all the time luck. [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, my gosh. That's all right. Go ahead. And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I arrived there with this piece of paper. All of a sudden, I became Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you spoke the language.

speaker: interviewee
text: I spoke the language. But everybody around there knew me as Jewish from Rogalin, from Ostrog. They knew me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But they were Germans from Luxembourg. And they told me openly don't be afraid. We'll never give you out. We know who you are. But we won't denounce you. We don't like Hitler, as you don't. But there were a couple of Poles. [INTERPOSING VOICES] That was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: And you believed them? You believed those Germans who told you that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't have any choice. There were a couple of Poles which knew me, too. But they-- I was lucky from the beginning. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I stayed there in Rovno. And the, the manager there was very nice. His wife was really sweet-- a German. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She gave me the first slip to wear. She was a very sweet girl-- really nice. And he was, he was OK. He was scared, because you know what happened. There was a law that anybody who hides any Jew can be shot or put in a concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they hid you anyway.

speaker: interviewee
text: He hid me anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you stay there with them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed with them. I worked in the canteen serving food to the workers, being scared to open the door, look through the window, because I was always afraid somebody might recognize me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that was in '41-- still in '41 when I came there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And somehow, you know, one day after another went through. I don't know how long. But by the end of the year-- it must have been November when one of the drivers from the company decided to ask me for a date out. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was so scared to open the door. He asked me for a date out. So I refused. And because I refused, he went to the Gestapo and denounced me. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And one evening, three tall Germans, Gestapos, came, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] surrounded me, start asking questions-- tall guys. I had to look up to them. I think I spoke in broken German. Until today, I remember that in connection I don't know what it was. But instead of brot, which is German, I said Yiddish, broit. That stuck in my mind. They were asking-- they-- asking--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they catch it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It looks like no. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were asking where I was born. And at one time, they sent me for my papers. And me not thinking of anything, doing everything automatic, I went to the back room there, where I slept, took out this piece of paper, and came back. And they started asking questions from the paper-- which school I went, what was the name of the street, what was the name of the school. And I was lying, lying. Everything through my teeth, I was lying. I didn't know even what I was saying. And after a while, from nowhere, they went. They let me free-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] luck number one of three, four. Nobody--

speaker: interviewer
text: Because he didn't really know you were Jewish-- the one who asked you out on a date? Or he did know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the Gestapo questioned you when they--

speaker: interviewee
text: But he wasn't there.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they believed you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They believed me. But whoever heard a Gestapo believing? First of all, from other experience, what I know, they take you down to the headquarters and start interrogating you there. The three of them went back and let me go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after that was very bad, because my-- the manager, this boss of mine, was so scared he came out after this white. He was-- and I couldn't blame him. He was so scared. And at that point, he told me you just have to go. Go where?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I waited a couple of days. And luck number five or six or something, he had a friend which visited him frequently there, who was really in the brown uniform, he was an employee of the national bank, German bank, in the brown uniform with the Hakenkreuz here. There was the NS-- NSDAP or something, a Nazi. But they were friends. And he was from Berlin, stationed, so to say, in Rovno working in the bank. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So a couple of days later, all of a sudden, he asked me if I would like to go with him to Berlin, because his wife is going to have a third child, and she needs help. It's like-- Did he know you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Somebody sent him down from up there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because I really-- I was looking to join the partisans. But I didn't know where to go. I didn't know which direction to go, nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I didn't have any contact with anybody. So he came, really, like a salvation. If my boss inspired him to it, I don't know. He wanted to get rid of me, I know. But that came as a blessing. So he started working on the papers to take me to Germany. I remember his wife sent for me. I didn't have any clothes. He sent for me a pair of nylon stockings for me to wear. And I had a problem because it was cold already towards December. And I didn't have a coat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he took a blanket, a gray blanket, and wanted me to go to a seamstress to make a coat. And I didn't have any choice. I had to go. I was so scared to go out on this street of Rovno. I was so scared. I was looking, you know, over my shoulders. But I had to go. I couldn't say I don't want to go. I had to give him a reason. So she made me a gray coat. And in December [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] '41, which was almost '42, I was traveling to Berlin with all the biggest SS men, because that was a special assigned train for the good SS to go back to Germany to spend their vacation with their families. So that was a luxury train, heated train full of food, drinks, everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: My boss from Rovno went on this train, too, and his wife. They knew all along I'm Jewish. But others didn't know. So I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I, I really can't believe it. I was traveling [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in a luxury train in December going to Berlin, train all full SS men in their uniforms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think it was an overnight ride. Don't ask me how I did it, but I did it. Everything came just automatic without thinking. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I arrived to his house in Berlin, which was the outskirts of Berlin. It was called Woltersdorf Schleuse. I don't even know how to spell this, Woltersdorf, because that was kind of a-- it was on the, on a lake outside of Berlin, a town located on a lake. So Sundays, the Germans from Berlin used to come there and have coffee around the lake. That was for them like a, an excursion. And the house, I remember, was located on Berliner Strasse 89. That I remember. And I got into this household where my new boss-- his name was also Erwin Hadrian, H-A-D-R-I-A-N, Hadrian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how, how long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was already '42 . So I stayed there till '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Two years? While the baby was born and--

speaker: interviewee
text: While the baby was born. He had two children. The older one was [NON-ENGLISH]. The younger one was [NON-ENGLISH]. He had a old-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] his sister, a spinster, an old maid, lived with them. And she was terrible. His wife was wonderful, very, very nice, decent woman. I think before the war, she was cleaning houses for the Jewish Germans. But she was very nice, very decent. She was afraid of him, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because when he went in the morning out to his office or so, she was listening to the BBC. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she had part of the family, which was visiting Sundays. Sundays was always a special meal with rabbit. That was the treat. And I used to go, actually, from Berlin-- oh, when I arrived in Berlin, the first thing I got is a P letter. I was supposed to wear that P for Polish. I was supposed to wear outside the coat or whatever I wear and underneath, too. And I used to go to Berlin with the two girls by streetcar, it's so near. But I always took off the P.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to-- because they didn't look so friendly at foreigners. And I used to go with those two of his daughters to Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: By this time, were you speaking German?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you know the war was winding down? Were you aware?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, didn't know anything-- didn't know anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were there for two years till 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: For two years in this household. I had a little room in the attic, which was just room for a bed. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But I helped her clean the house. And Sundays, I could go with the girls to Berlin. And I remember one accident. I was so scared in Berlin, even, that somebody might see me, recognize me, that I was once on the street. And I thought somebody recognized me. And I jumped in so fast to a streetcar so I smashed my finger. I have a flat finger from that time. And also, when I used to go with the two girls, and I didn't have the P-- so in the streetcar once, I remember an SS man in uniform got up and give me the seat. And I remember the girl, the older girl, Margot, telling me if he would know that you're Polish, he would never give you the seat.

speaker: interviewer
text: And if he would know you were Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: And if he would know that I'm Jewish, I wouldn't be there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so what happened after the two-year period?

speaker: interviewee
text: So at the same time, everybody was suspicious, because I never had any contact with anybody-- no letters. They kept asking, where is your family? Where is-- I said, nobody's there. So I got friends with another Polish family, a shoemaker, where every Sunday, they used to-- the Poles used to get together. And I spent a couple of hours just to get out and to see that I have somebody. I think I even used to write  a letter to myself just to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] take them off track. He had one-- he was such a Nazi, this boss of mine, that I remember one evening he came for dinner. And he was so proud that he caught another [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anti-Nazi, because he was showing I finished him-- like this. He had one aunt used to visit them sometimes. And she was strongly against Hitler to the point that she had a son. And she didn't let him go to the Hitler Jugend that all the young people had to go. And for that, she was punished. They sent him to the Russian front, her son. And she kept him-- she always used to tell me, Janina, you just don't look like a maid. And that I heard very often. And every time I heard it, I got very, very scared. Every time the bell rang in the evening, I thought, here they come. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was there two years going Sunday sometimes to this Poles. There was even a Pole that wanted to marry me. [INAUDIBLE]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I could have had it easier, maybe. But I didn't.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I was there till after near two years [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there came an order that no German can employ a foreigner. It was a law. They needed people in the factories very badly. So that's how they sent me to this labor camp in Kremmen, which--

speaker: interviewer
text: As a Pole.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a Pole, yeah. Which was about [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two hours, maybe, by train. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So I arrived to this camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They put me in a room with other Polish girls-- one room, over 30 of us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And there were all kinds of those girls, because they used to take them from street, send them to the factories in Germany. So you could find there from the very educated to the lowest.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was one little oval in the center-- how do you call it? It's from [INAUDIBLE]. Little stove. Like a stove?

speaker: interviewee
text: A little stove with one burner. And there were always fighting going on, who's going to cook the potatoes first.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you lived there and worked there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there every day. We went with the-- one of the Germans took us into the factory from the barracks-- there used to be two beds, one on top of the other and straw-- and bring us back in the evening. And every evening they used to check if-- but the girls used to steal parachute material.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To make underwear, dresses. They used them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were you doing there? You said you were making parachutes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was making parachutes, folding parachutes, which small. And they were attached to the light bulbs for the German pilots to send before a raid. Before they threw the bombs in the evening, they always put those lights first on to see better. That was our job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did you stay at that work camp, at that labor camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the labor camp, I stayed till the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me what that was like.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the labor camp, I didn't take part any fights. I didn't cook. I kept obviously a low profile. And they used to call me the black Janina, [NON-ENGLISH], because my hair was black. But none of the 30-something Polish girls was suspicious that I'm Jewish. I didn't have any accident.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were very lucky.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very. All through I was lucky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, you were.

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- I, I start-- I-- since this war, I really believe in destiny, because somebody was really watching over me step by step by step.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember what happened when you were liberated?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't reach it yet. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were Polish. The nearby were Italian girls. Supposedly, they came voluntarily. But I never know. There were some French prisoners in the same camp, which were treated according to the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Geneva Convention?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really?

speaker: interviewee
text: They got care packages. They were treated good. They had chocolate, cigarettes, everything. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There were some Italian prisoners, which were the ones which were fighting against, the Badoglio soldiers, which were treated terrible-- worse than us-- terrible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was there in '44 until one morning. We didn't know anything was going on. I didn't even know in Berlin when I was that the biggest concentration camp for political prisoners was right there not far. Oranienburg was a famous concentration camp-- Oranienburg. And I didn't know it. I know only-- when I was in this German household, I know only workers. They work on the road. And my-- the, the wife of my boss was so nice. She used to send me out with some bread for them. She was really very nice. I wish I could see her. Anyway, when I arrived to Kremmen, to the labor camp, I kept in touch with those Polish people that used to write me letters-- anti-Semitic letters. In one of the letters, she said it's so noisy here, like in a Jewish cheder-- this friend of mine. She didn't know I'm Jewish. And while being there, I got the letter that a month after I left, the bomb hit the house-- one month after I left. By the way, when I was in this household, we were most of the time in the cellar, in the basement, because they were bombarding a little town nearby on the name of Erkner, E-R-K-N-E-R, because they had some industry there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was anybody hurt when the house was bombed? You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't-- I, I lost contact with them. I don't know. I have no idea. The house went down. And I don't know what happened to the people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I tried to get in touch after the war with this boss of mine from Rovno because I thought he was from Hamburg. I was in Hamburg. And so I couldn't find him. They moved. Anyway, being in labor camp, one day in the morning we got out, no patrol, nobody at the door, open. The Germans which were patrolling us ran.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we knew the war is almost to the end. So everybody from the camp started running. The problem is we ran out from the camps. And there were planes coming down shooting almost from machine guns from the planes. They came so low. So while we were running, all the Germans were running towards-- I think towards the west. They knew where the Americans are.

speaker: interviewer
text: They wanted the Americans to capture them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Absolutely. So while we were running, we had to always hide in the ditches, you know, on the road. Every two, three minutes, another plane, it was shooting. I mean, why were they shooting at the civilians? I have no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you see which planes they were?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Russian planes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, we'll continue what happened when you were liberated in just one minute, please.
